articles.title: Tiger Woods in a Car Crash
articles.summary: Tiger Woods, a famous golfer, was in a car crash in Florida. He rolled his car after trying to pass a truck too fast. No one got hurt, but he was arrested because police thought he might be impaired by something, not alcohol. He took a breath test and passed it, but he refused another test. He stayed in jail for eight hours and then was released on bail. This means he can go home but must follow rules until his court date. The crash caused damage to property, and he faces charges for driving under the influence and refusing the test.

articles.title: Fun Easter Treats: Are They Too Crazy?
articles.summary: Easter is a time for yummy treats like creme eggs and hot cross buns. These are special foods people eat to celebrate. But now, stores are selling new kinds with different flavors. For example, creme eggs might have Biscoff or white chocolate inside. Hot cross buns can be red velvet, lemon curd, or tiramisu. Some people, like Ethan, love trying all the new flavors. Others, like Jann, prefer the old-fashioned ones with just fruit. The article asks if these creative treats are good or if we should stick to the basics, especially when money is tight. It talks to shoppers to see what they think.

articles.title: Austria Wants to Keep Kids Off Social Media
articles.summary: Austria is planning a new rule to stop children under 14 from using social media. This means apps like TikTok or Instagram would not be allowed for young kids. The government says this will protect children from bad things online and too much screen time. They want to use special age checks to make sure only older people can sign up. Other countries like Australia and France are doing similar bans for kids. Austria also plans to teach kids in school about using media safely. The rule needs approval from lawmakers before it starts.

articles.title: Young and Old Conservatives Disagree on War in Iran
articles.summary: This article talks about how some people in America are worried about a war in Iran. Most Americans do not like this war, but many Republicans still support their president. At a big meeting called CPAC in Texas, people shared their thoughts. Older conservatives often support the war, but younger ones are not sure. They ask questions like why the war started and how it will end. Young people like Toby and Shashank think the war costs too much money and hurts Americans at home. They want the president to focus on problems in the US instead. The war is causing a split between older and younger conservatives, making it hard for everyone to agree.

articles.title: A Year After Government Cuts: Workers Ask What Was Saved
articles.summary: One year ago, President Trump made big cuts to the government to save money. This affected many workers, like Thea Price, who worked at the United States Institute of Peace. She and 300 others were fired, then rehired, then fired again, which made their lives very hard. The cuts were led by a group called DOGE, which wanted to stop waste in the government. DOGE says it saved $215 billion by cutting jobs and other things, but over 260,000 workers lost their jobs. Now, people like Thea are asking if the savings were worth all the trouble and pain they went through. The article shows that while some money was saved, the human cost was high, leaving workers confused and hurt.

articles.title: Congress Disagrees on Homeland Security Money
articles.summary: In Congress, the Senate passed a plan to end the shutdown for most of the Department of Homeland Security, which helps keep the country safe. But House Speaker Mike Johnson said no because the plan didn't include money for ICE and Border Patrol, who work at the borders. Now, House Republicans want a different plan, which might make the shutdown last longer. Meanwhile, President Trump used an executive order to pay TSA workers at airports, who check bags and people for safety. Some TSA workers have quit because they haven't been paid, and they feel tired of being used in political fights. The shutdown is causing problems like long lines at airports.
